stem: Inferior scapular angle is at which level?
subject: Anatomy
explanation:
Solution
The inferior angle of the scapula is located at the level of the 7th thoracic vertebra T7-T8.

Some of the notable bony markings on the scapula include:
1. Acromion process - located at the lateral end of the scapular spine, it articulates with the clavicle to form the acromioclavicular joint.
2. Coracoid process - a hook-shaped projection that projects anteriorly from the superior border of the scapula, it serves as an attachment point for various muscles and ligaments.
3. Glenoid cavity - a shallow, concave depression on the lateral angle of the scapula, it articulates with the head of the humerus to form the glenohumeral joint.
4. Scapular spine - a bony ridge that runs diagonally across the posterior surface of the scapula.
5. Supraspinous fossa - a concave depression above the scapular spine, it provides attachment for the supraspinatus muscle.
6. Infraspinous fossa - a concave depression below the scapular spine, it provides attachment for the infraspinatus muscle.
7. Subscapular fossa - a broad, shallow concavity on the anterior surface of the scapula, it provides attachment for the subscapularis muscle.

Other Options:
Option A:
Suprasternal notch is present at T2 level.
Option B:
Sternal angle is present at T4 level.
Option C:
Upper border of the liver is present at T6 level.

stem: Identify the marked tendon in the diagram
subject: Anatomy, Orthopaedics
explanation:
Solution
The given above image points towards the tendon of extensor hallucis longus.
Extensor hallucis longus:-
The extensor hallucis longus (EHL) is a long, thin muscle located on the anterior (front) aspect of the leg. It originates from the upper two-thirds of the fibula bone and the interosseous membrane (a strong, fibrous tissue that connects the tibia and fibula bones).
Action:-
This tendon is responsible for extending (lifting up) the great toe at the metatarsophalangeal (MTP) joint (where the toe meets the foot) and at the interphalangeal (IP) joint (where the toe bones meet).
Innervation
The EHL is innervated by the deep peroneal nerve, which also supplies other muscles in the anterior compartment of the leg.

Other Options:
Option B:
It's tendon comes from below the foot and not visible over the dorsum.
Option C:
It does not have its insertion to the great toe rather than other toes.
Option D:
Tendon of peroneus longus comes from the lateral side of foot and crosses the dorsum.

stem: Which of the following gland is supplied by the glossopharyngeal nerve?
subject: Anatomy
explanation:
Solution
• The parotid gland is supplied by the glossopharyngeal nerve through its secretomotor fibers (parasympathetic supply).
• The fibers from the inferior salivatory nucleus of the IXth nerve travel to the inferior ganglion through jugular foramen, from where it reaches tympanic plexus via tympanic branch and continues as lesser Petrosal nerve till the otic ganglion.
In the otic ganglion, the fibers synapse and travel along the auriculotemporal nerve to supply the parotid gland.

Other Options:
Option A:
Submandibular salivary gland: The submandibular gland is innervated by both sympathetic and parasympathetic fibers. The parasympathetic innervation comes from the chorda tympani branch of the facial nerve (CN VII), while the sympathetic fibers come from the superior cervical ganglion.
Option B:
Lacrimal gland: The lacrimal gland is innervated by the parasympathetic fibers of the facial nerve (CN VII). The fibers pass through the greater petrosal nerve and then synapse in the pterygopalatine ganglion. Postganglionic fibers from the ganglion then reach the lacrimal gland.
Option D:
It gets supply through chorda tympani of facial nerve.

stem: An injury to the shown area can lead to fracture of which bone:
subject: Anatomy, Orthopaedics
explanation:
Solution
The area shown in the image is the anatomical snuff box. The main bone present in its floor is the Scaphoid.
• Therefore, an injury to this area leads mostly to the fracture of the Scaphoid bone.
Anatomical snuffbox:
• Boundaries:
• The anatomical snuff box is bounded laterally by the tendons of abductor pollicis longus and extensor pollicis brevis, medially by extensor pollicis longus, and the floor is formed by Scaphoid and Trapezium.
• Content:
The contents of the snuff box are the Radial artery and dorsal cutaneous branch (Radial nerve) and cephalic vein.

Other Options:
Option A: Lunate is the second carpal bone and is neither a boundary nor a content of the anatomical snuffbox.
Option C: Hamate is the last capital bone and is neither a boundary nor a content of the anatomical snuffbox.
Option D: Trapezius is the fifth carpal bone and forms the floor of the anatomical snuffbox along with scaphoid but is not as likely to be fractured as scaphoid.

stem: Mammillary body is a structure on the undersurface of the brain, developing from the diencephalon, that forms a part of the limbic system. They receive afferent fibers via the:
subject: Anatomy
explanation:
Solution
The mammillary bodies receive afferent fibers via the fornix.
The mammillary bodies are a pair of small, round structures located on the underside of the brain, specifically within the hypothalamus.
They are involved in the processing and storage of memory, particularly spatial memory, and are considered part of the limbic system.
• The fornix is a C-shaped bundle of nerve fibers that connects the hippocampus to the mammillary bodies and other parts of the limbic system.

Other Options:
Option A:
The corpus callosum is a large band of nerve fibers that connects the two hemispheres of the brain and does not have direct connections with the mammillary bodies.
Option B:
The thalamus is a relay center for sensory and motor information and is not directly involved in connecting with the mammillary bodies.
Option C:
The pituitary gland, located in the sella turcica of the sphenoid bone, is involved in the regulation of the endocrine system and does not have direct connections with the mammillary bodies.

stem: Trismus is due to spasm of which of the following muscle
subject: Anatomy, ENT
explanation:
Solution
Trismus, also known as lockjaw, is due to spasm of the muscles of mastication, specifically the medial pterygoid and temporalis muscles. Therefore, option C is the correct answer.
• The medial pterygoid muscle is one of the muscles of mastication, which is responsible for elevating the mandible during chewing.
• Spasm of this muscle can cause trismus, or difficulty opening the mouth.
• The temporalis muscle, another muscle of mastication, is responsible for retracting and elevating the mandible, and spasm of this muscle can also cause trismus.

Other Options:
Option A:
The buccinator muscle is responsible for compressing the cheeks against the teeth and is not directly involved in opening or closing the jaw.
Option B:
The lateral pterygoid muscle is responsible for depressing and protracting the mandible during chewing and does not contribute to trismus.
Option D:
The mentalis muscle is responsible for elevating and protruding the lower lip and chin and is not involved in the movement of the mandible.

stem: Nitric oxide is derived from:
subject: Biochemistry, Physiology
explanation:
Solution
• Nitric oxide (NO) is derived from Arginine.
• NO is produced by the enzyme nitric oxide synthase (NOS), which converts arginine into NO and citrulline.
• NO is an important signaling molecule that plays a key role in regulating blood pressure, neurotransmission, and immune function.

Other Options:
Option B: Histidine is not directly involved in the synthesis of nitric oxide. However, it is an essential amino acid that is involved in the biosynthesis of various other important molecules in the body.
Option C: Glycine is also not directly involved in the synthesis of nitric oxide. It is a non-essential amino acid that plays a role in the biosynthesis of other important molecules, such as glutathione.
Option D: Aspartate is also not directly involved in the synthesis of nitric oxide. It is an amino acid that is involved in the biosynthesis of other important molecules, such as nucleotides and certain neurotransmitters.

stem: Which of the following is cardio protective?
subject: Biochemistry, Medicine
explanation:
Solution
HDL (high-density lipoprotein) is cardio-protective. HDL is commonly referred to as "good cholesterol" because it helps remove excess cholesterol from the blood vessels and transport it back to the liver for processing and excretion.
High levels of HDL in the blood are associated with a lower risk of cardiovascular disease. Therefore, Option A is the correct answer.

Other Options:
Option B: LDL (Low-density lipoprotein) is often referred to as "bad cholesterol" because it can build up in the walls of arteries and form plaque, which can narrow and eventually block blood flow. LDL has a higher proportion of cholesterol to protein.
Option C: IDL (Intermediate-density lipoprotein) is a lipoprotein that is intermediate in size between LDL and VLDL. It is formed when VLDL loses some of its triglycerides and becomes more cholesterol-rich.
Option D: VLDL (Very-low-density lipoprotein) is a type of lipoprotein that carries triglycerides, a type of fat, from the liver to other parts of the body.
• Elevated levels of VLDL are associated with increased triglyceride levels and may be indicative of dyslipidemia, a condition characterized by abnormal lipid levels in the blood.
High levels of VLDL and triglycerides, along with low levels of HDL cholesterol, are commonly seen in conditions such as metabolic syndrome and can increase the risk of cardiovascular disease.

stem: Which structure of Protein is not denatured after heating upto100 degree?
subject: Biochemistry
explanation:
Solution
• The primary structure of protein is not denatured after heating up to 100 degrees Celsius.
• The primary structure of a protein refers to its specific sequence of amino acids, which is held together by peptide bonds.
Heating a protein can cause the weaker intermolecular forces, such as hydrogen bonds and van der Waals forces, to break down and cause the protein to denature.
However, the covalent peptide bonds that form the primary structure of the protein are much stronger and are not easily broken by heat alone.
• Therefore, the primary structure remains intact even after heating up to 100 degrees Celsius.

Other Options:
Option B: The secondary structure of a protein refers to the local folding patterns of the polypeptide chain, such as alpha-helices and beta-sheets. Heating a protein can disrupt the hydrogen bonds and other non-covalent interactions that stabilize the secondary structure, resulting in its denaturation.
Option C: The tertiary structure of a protein refers to the overall three-dimensional shape of the polypeptide chain, including the folding of secondary structural elements and the interactions between different regions of the protein. Heating a protein can cause the disruption of hydrophobic interactions, hydrogen bonds, and disulfide bonds that stabilize the tertiary structure, resulting in its denaturation.
Option D: The quaternary structure of a protein refers to the interactions between multiple polypeptide chains in a protein complex. Heating a protein can cause the disruption of the non-covalent interactions that stabilize the quaternary structure, resulting in its denaturation.

stem: Melanin is derived from which amino acid?
subject: Biochemistry
explanation:
Solution
Melanin is a pigment that is responsible for the color of our skin, hair, and eyes. It is produced by specialized cells called melanocytes, which are located in the skin and other tissues.
The synthesis of melanin begins with the amino acid tyrosine, which is converted to dihydroxyphenylalanine (DOPA) by the enzyme tyrosinase.
• DOPA is then further converted to dopaquinone, which is the precursor to both eumelanin (brown/black pigment) and pheomelanin (red/yellow pigment).
• The exact type of melanin produced by melanocytes depends on a variety of factors, including genetics and environmental factors.

Other Options:
Option B: Tryptophan is also an amino acid, but it is not directly involved in the synthesis of melanin. Tryptophan is a precursor to other important molecules in the body, including serotonin and melatonin.
Option C: Phenylalanine is an essential amino acid that is involved in the synthesis of other important molecules, including neurotransmitters and hormones.
Option D: Alanine is a non-essential amino acid that is primarily involved in protein synthesis and metabolism, and is not involved in the synthesis of melanin.

stem: Which of the following is present in skeletal muscle?
subject: Biochemistry, Physiology
explanation:
Solution
GLUT 4 is present in skeletal muscle.
• It is responsible for insulin-mediated glucose uptake into these tissues. When insulin binds to its receptor on the surface of skeletal muscle cells, it activates a signaling cascade that promotes the translocation of GLUT 4 from intracellular vesicles to the plasma membrane.
This increases the number of glucose transporters on the cell surface, allowing for increased glucose uptake and utilization by the muscle cells.

Other Options:
Option B: GLUT 5 is primarily found in the small intestine and is responsible for fructose uptake.
Option C: GLUT 7 is found in the small intestine and liver.
Option D: GLUT 2 is found in the liver, pancreas, and small intestine.

stem: Synovial fluid contains;
subject: Biochemistry, Orthopaedics
explanation:
Solution
• Synovial fluid is a clear, viscous fluid found in joints, which provides lubrication and nutrition to the joint tissues. It is primarily composed of hyaluronic acid, a large glycosaminoglycan molecule that helps to maintain the viscosity and lubrication of the synovial fluid.

Other Options:
Option B: Chondroitin sulphate is a glycosaminoglycan (GAG) that is found in the extracellular matrix of cartilage, where it helps to provide structure and support. It is a long, linear molecule made up of repeating units of glucuronic acid and N-acetylgalactosamine.
Option C: Keratan sulphate is another type of GAG that is found in cartilage, as well as other tissues such as the cornea of the eye. It is a long, linear molecule made up of repeating units of galactose and N-acetylglucosamine. Like chondroitin sulphate, keratan sulphate helps to provide structure and support to connective tissues.
Option D: Dermatan sulphate is a GAG that is found in the extracellular matrix of skin, blood vessels, and other tissues. It is a long, linear molecule made up of repeating units of iduronic acid and N-acetylgalactosamine.

stem: The bases which are present in human DNA?
subject: Biochemistry
explanation:
Solution
Explanation: Human DNA is made up of four different nitrogenous bases: adenine (A), guanine (G), cytosine (C), and thymine (T).
These bases pair up in a specific way: A always pairs with T. and C always pairs with G. This base pairing is the basis for the double helix structure of DNA.
Uracil is present in RNA only.

Other Options:
Option A: Uracil (U) is a nitrogenous base that is found in RNA (ribonucleic acid) instead of thymine.
Option B: Uracil (U) is a nitrogenous base that is found In RNA (ribonucleic acid) instead of thymine.
Option D: None of the above is not correct.

stem: A 2-week-old Infant is diagnosed with a urea cycle defect. Enzymatic analysis showed no activity for ornithine transcarbamylase (OTC). Molecular analysis revealed that the mRNA product of the gene for OTC was identical to that of a control. Which of the techniques listed below was most likely used to analyze the size and amount of the mRNA?
subject: Biochemistry, Pediatrics
explanation:
Solution
• The most likely technique that was used to analyze the size and amount of mRNA is Northern blot.
• Northern blot is a laboratory technique used to study gene expression by detecting RNA molecules.
It involves the separation of RNA molecules by size through gel electrophoresis, followed by transfer of the RNA to a membrane.
The RNA is then hybridized with a labeled DNA probe, which is complementary to the RNA sequence of interest.
The labeled RNA is then detected using autoradiography or other imaging methods, allowing the size and amount of the RNA to be determined.

Other Options:
Option A: Dideoxy chain termination is a technique used in DNA sequencing. It involves the use of modified nucleotides that terminate DNA chain elongation during replication, resulting in a set of DNA fragments of different lengths. This technique is not used for analyzing the size and amount of mRNA
Option C: Western blotting is a technique used to study protein expression and detect specific proteins in a sample, It involves the separation of proteins by gel electrophoresis, followed by transfer of the proteins to a membrane and incubation with a labeled antibody that recognizes the protein of interest.
Option D: Polymerase chain reaction (PCR) is a technique used to amplify a specific DNA sequence. It involves the use of primers that anneal to the ends of the target sequence, followed by the amplification of the sequence by a DNA polymerase. This technique is not used for analyzing the size and amount of mRNA.

stem: Atherosclerosis involves the formation of lipid-rich plaques in the intima of arteries which can narrow the coronary arteries, leading to decreased blood flow and hypoxia of cardiac cells. Which of the following amino acid is known to be associated with Atherosclerosis?
subject: Biochemistry, Pathology, Medicine
explanation:
Solution
Homocysteine is the amino acid that is known to be associated with atherosclerosis.
Elevated levels of homocysteine in the blood have been linked to an increased risk of cardiovascular disease, including atherosclerosis, which is the buildup of plaque in the arteries.

Other Options:
Option A: Methionine is another sulfur-containing amino acid, but it has not been directly linked to atherosclerosis.
Option B: Cysteine is also a sulfur-containing amino acid that plays a role in the formation of disulfide bonds in proteins, but it is not known to be associated with atherosclerosis.
Option D: Leucine is a branched-chain amino acid that is involved in protein synthesis and muscle growth, but it is not associated with atherosclerosis.

stem: Km increases, but Vmax remains the same. This is a type of Inhibition:
subject: Biochemistry
explanation:
Solution
Explanation:
• Competitive inhibitors compete with the substrate for binding to the active site of the enzyme and can be overcome by Increasing the substrate concentration.
• This results in an increase in Km but does not affect Vmax.

Other Options:
Option B: Non-competitive Inhibitors bind to a site on the enzyme other than the active site and can decrease Vmax but do not affect Km.
Option C: Irreversible inhibitors bind covalently to the enzyme and permanently Inactivate it, resulting in a decrease in Vmax but not necessarily affecting Km.
Option D: In uncompetitive inhibition, the inhibitor molecule binds to the enzyme-substrate complex, preventing the release of the product and reducing the overall rate of the reaction. Uncompetitive inhibitors decrease both the Vmax and the Km of the reaction, because they reduce both the affinity of the enzyme for the substrate and the maximum velocity at which the reaction can occur.

stem: Most Common enzyme deficient in galactosemics:
subject: Biochemistry, Pediatrics
explanation:
Solution
The most common enzyme deficient in galactosemia is galactose-1-phosphate uridyltransferase (GALT), which is involved in the conversion of galactose-1-phosphate to glucose-1-phosphate in the liver.
GALT deficiency leads to the accumulation of galactose-1-phosphate and other galactose metabolites, which can cause liver damage, cataracts, and intellectual disability.

Other Options:
Option B: Galactosidase is an enzyme that catalyzes the hydrolysis of lactose into glucose and galactose. Deficiency in galactosidase leads to lactose intolerance, a common condition in which the body is unable to digest lactose due to a lack of functional galactosidase. • However, galactosidase deficiency is not related to galactosemia, which is caused by a deficiency in GALT.
Option C: • UDP galactose epimerase is an enzyme that catalyzes the interconversion of UDP-galactose and UDP-glucose, two Important sugars involved in carbohydrate metabolism, • Deficiency in UDP galactose epimerase is a rare genetic disorder known as epimerase deficiency galactosemia, which is characterized by elevated levels of galactose and galactose-1-phosphate in the blood and tissues.
Option D: Galactokinase is an enzyme that catalyzes the conversion of galactose to galactose-1-phosphate, a key step in galactose metabolism. • Deficiency in galactokinase leads to a rare genetic disorder known as galactokinase deficiency galactosemia, which is characterized by elevated levels of galactose in the blood and tissues.

stem: Most common primary site for brain metastasis is which of the following?
subject: Pathology, Medicine
explanation:
Solution
Explanation: Lung cancer is the correct answer.
It is the most common primary site for brain metastasis.
Brain metastases occur when cancer cells from a primary lung tumor spread to the brain.
• The brain is a common site of metastasis for lung cancer due to the rich blood supply to the lungs and the proximity of the brain to the lungs.
• Metastatic lesions, mostly carcinomas, account for approximately one-fourth to one-half of intracranial tumors in hospitalized patients.
The five most common primary sites are lung, breast, skin (melanoma), kidney, and gastrointestinal tract, accounting for about 80% of all metastases combined.

Other Options:
Option B: Head and neck cancer It can also metastasize to the brain, but it is less common compared to lung cancer. Head and neck cancers include various types of cancers that originate in the mouth, throat, nose, sinuses, and other nearby structures. • When head and neck cancer cells break away from the primary tumor and enter the bloodstream or lymphatic system, they can potentially spread to distant sites, including the brain.
Option C: Prostate cancer • Prostate cancer is more likely to metastasize to bones, especially the spine and pelvis, rather than the brain. Brain metastasis from prostate cancer is relatively uncommon compared to other sites of metastasis.
Option D: Breast cancer Breast cancer can also metastasize to the brain. It is one of the more common primary sites for brain metastasis after lung cancer. • Breast cancer cells can enter the bloodstream or lymphatic system and travel to distant organs, including the brain, leading to the formation of brain metastases.

stem: The following serological status is noted in a patient: HbsAg positive and HbeAg positive. Diagnosis is?
subject: Microbiology, Medicine
explanation:
Solution
Explanation:
• HBsAg appears before the onset of symptoms and peaks in acute, symptomatic disease. In those who clear the infection, it often declines to undetectable levels in 12 weeks, but can be present for as long as 24 weeks. By contrast, HBsAg persists in cases that progress to chronicity.
• HBeAg, HBV DNA, and HBV DNA polymerase are detectable in the serum soon after HBsAg and signify active viral replication.

Other Options:
Option A: Acute viral hepatitis Acute viral hepatitis refers to the initial stage of a viral hepatitis infection, which can be caused by different viruses, including hepatitis A, B, C, D, and E. It is characterized by the acute onset of liver inflammation due to viral replication in the liver cells. Symptoms may include fatigue, jaundice (yellowing of the skin and eyes), loss of appetite, nausea, vomiting, and abdominal pain. In the case of hepatitis B, the presence of HbsAg and HbeAg indicates an acute infection with active viral replication.
Option B: Chronic viral hepatitis It refers to a long-term or persistent infection of the hepatitis virus, typically lasting for more than six months. Chronic hepatitis can result from infections with hepatitis B, C, or D. Chronic hepatitis B is associated with the persistent presence of HbsAg. • However, in chronic hepatitis B, the HbeAg is often negative or may be positive but Indicates a lower level of viral replication compared to the acute phase. In this context, the patient's HbeAg positive status suggests an active infection, which is not indicative of chronic hepatitis.
Option D: Remote Infection "Remote Infection" is not a standard medical term used in the context of hepatitis or viral infections. It does not provide a clear explanation of the patient's current serological status. As the patient is positive for both HbsAg and HbeAg, It suggests an active and ongoing infection, which contradicts the notion of a "remote" or past infection.

stem: Most common anterior mediastinal tumor?
subject: Surgery, Pathology, Radiology
explanation:
Solution
Explanation: Most common anterior mediastinal tumor is thymoma.
The thymus is a gland located in the anterior mediastinum, which is the central compartment of the chest between the lungs.
Thymomas are tumors that originate from the epithelial cells of the thymus gland.
They are generally slow-growing and can be either benign (non-cancerous) or malignant (cancerous).

Other Options:
Option B: Neurofibroma It is a tumor that originates from nerve sheath cells. • While neurofibromas can occur in various parts of the body, including the mediastinum, they are not as common as thymomas in the anterior mediastinum.
Option C: Pericardial cysts • These are benign fluid-filled sacs that develop in the pericardium, the membrane surrounding the heart. • They are not true tumors but rather congenital cysts, and they are not as common as thymomas in the anterior mediastinum.
Option D: Bronchogenic cysts These are congenital abnormalities that can occur anywhere along the respiratory tract, including the mediastinum. • While they can be found in the anterior mediastinum, they are not as common as thymomas.

stem: A 45-year patient working in a factory for the past 20 years presents with breathlessness. HRCT chest shows pleural thickening and fibrosis. What is the person suffering from?
subject: PSM, Medicine, Radiology
explanation:
Solution
Explanation:
• Asbestos is a family of proinflammatory crystalline hydrated silicates that are associated with pulmonary fibrosis and various forms of cancer.
Given the patient's occupation of working in a factory for the past 20 years and the preserice of pleural thickening and fibrosis on HRCT chest, asbestosis is the most likely diagnosis.
The prolonged exposure to asbestos fibers in the factory environment has resulted in lung damage and respiratory symptoms like breathlessness.

Other Options:
Option B: Coal workers pneumoconiosis • Coal workers' pneumoconiosis (CWP), commonly known as "black lung disease," occurs when coal dust is inhaled. It is a lung disease caused by inhalation of coal dust. It typically affects coal miners and is characterized by the accumulation of coal dust particles in the lungs, leading to inflammation and the formation of blackened lung tissue. Pleural thickening and fibrosis are not common features of CWP.
Option C: Silicosis Silicosis is a long-term lung disease caused by inhaling large amounts of crystalline silica dust. It is often seen in occupations like mining, construction, and sandblasting. Silicosis can cause lung scarring, but pleural thickening is not a prominent feature.
Option D: Berylllosis • Berylliosis, also known as chronic beryllium disease (CBD), Is a granulomatous disease caused by exposure to beryllium. It can lead to granulomas and lung scarring, but it is a rare condition compared to asbestosis

stem: A 12-year-old Boy came with complaints of Severe Abdominal pain and headache for the past 2 days. H/O On & off for the past 1 week, associated with myalgia, and several episodes of vomiting self-medicated with Tab Paracetamol with no relief. Presents with Dengue NS-1 antigen (+). What is the first line of defense of the body in the given scenario?
subject: Microbiology, Pathology
explanation:
Solution
Explanation: NK cell is a type of immune cell that has granules (small particles) with enzymes that can kill tumor cells or cells infected with a virus.

Other Options:
Option B: T cells are cd4 and cd8+CELLS.
Option C: Histiocyte is a normal immune cell that is found in many parts of the body especially in the bone marrow, the blood stream, the skin, the liver, the lungs, the lymph glands and the spleen.
Option D: Macrophage is type of white blood cell that surrounds and kills microorganisms, removes dead cells, and stimulates the action of other Immune system cells.

stem: A stable 25 years old farmer was admitted with acute onset of fever, cough, and dyspnea. Chest radiograph showed findings of pneumonia for which he was treated with antibiotics; showed good response. After 1 month he again presented in the hypoxemic state with progressive dyspnea. CT chest showed diffuse ground glass changes. Transbronchial biopsies showed numerous small granulomas and marked lymphocytosis. Which of the following is a causative agent for a given disease?
subject: Pathology, Medicine
explanation:
Solution
Explanation: • Farmer's lung results from exposure to dusts generated from humid, warm, newly harvested hay that permits the rapid proliferation of the spores of thermophilic actinomycetes They Include interstitial pneumonitis, consisting primarily of lymphocytes, plasma cells, and macrophages (eosinophils are rare), as well as non-necrotizing granulomas

Other Options:
Option B: • Invasive pulmonary aspergillosis is a serious infection with pneumonia. It can spread to other parts of the body
Option C: • Pulmonary actinomycosis is caused by certain bacteria normally found in the mouth and gastrointestinal tract.

stem: Antecedent diagnosis of Group A streptococcal infection in Acute rheumatic fever can be made by?
subject: Medicine, Pathology
explanation:
Solution
• Rheumatic fever (RF) is an acute, immunologically mediated, multisystem inflammatory disease classically occurring a few weeks after group A streptococcal pharyngitis.

Other Options:
Option B: CRP • C-reactive protein (CRP) is an acute phase reactant that increases in response to inflammation. While it can be elevated in various infiammatory conditions, it is not a specific marker for Group A streptococcal infection or the diagnosis of Acute rheumatic fever.
Option C: ESR elevation • Erythrocyte Sedimentation Rate (ESR) is another non-specific marker of inflammation. Like CRP, it can rise in many inflammatory conditions but doesn't directly indicate a Group A streptococcal infection or Acute rheumatic fever,
Option D: Low C3 levels • Low C3 levels may suggest the presence of a complement system deficiency, but it's not a direct indicator of Group A streptococcal infection or the diagnosis of Acute rheumatic fever.

stem: Correct about of Anaemia of chronic disease
options: Increased percentage of saturation of transferrin
subject: Pathology, Medicine
explanation:
Solution
Explanation: • Anemia of chronic disease is caused by inflammatory cytokines, which increase hepcidin levels, thereby reducing iron absorption and sequestering iron in macrophages, and also suppress erythropoietin production.

Other Options:
Option B: Increased percentage of saturation of transferrin • An increased percentage of saturation of transferrin indicates that more of the available iron-binding sites on transferrin are occupied by iron. This would be expected in a condition of iron overload, not in the context of anemia of chronic disease, Ferritin levels show storage iron. Transferrin saturation, checks how many places on your transferrin that can hold iron are actually doing so. Normal values are 15% to 50%. In severe cases of iron-deficiency and anemia, this number may fall below 10%. • The normal range for serum transferrin is between 250-300 mg/dL.
Option C: High transferrin High transferrin levels are generally seen in conditions of iron deficiency, where the body is trying to increase its capacity to transport iron. It's not a characteristic feature of anemia of chronic disease.
Option D: High serum Iron High serum iron levels are not typically associated with anemia of chronic disease. In this condition, iron is often sequestered within macrophages and not readily available in the serum for use in erythropoiesis.

stem: Which type of necrosis is seen in the brain?
subject: Pathology
explanation:
Solution
Explanation: • Liquefactive necrosis, in contrast to coagulative necrosis, Is characterized by digestion of the dead cells, resulting in transformation of the tissue into a viscous liquid. It is seen in focal bacterial or, occasionally, fungal infections, because microbes stimulate the accumulation of leukocytes and the liberation of enzymes from these cells.

Other Options:
Option C: Fat necrosis is death of fat tissue due to injury and loss of blood supply. It can occur from trauma or as a complication of surgery.
Option D: Fibrinold necrosis is a specific pattern of Irreversible, uncontrolled cell death that occurs when antigen-antibody complexes are deposited in the walls of vessels.

stem: Which of the following has Autosomal Recessive Inheritance?
subject: Pathology, Medicine
explanation:
Solution
Explanation: Sickle cell anemia is a genetic disorder caused by a mutation in the HBB gene. In an Autosomal Recessive inheritance pattern, both parents must pass on a copy of the mutated gene for the child to develop the disease. In the case of sickle cell anemia, an individual must inherit two coples of the abnormal hemoglobin gene (HbS) to have the condition. This genetic combination leads to the characteristic sickle-shaped red blood cells and various health complications associated with the disease.

Other Options:
Option A: Hereditary spherocytosis • Hereditary spherocytosis is primarily inherited in an autosomal dominant pattern, meaning that inheriting one copy of the mutated gene from either parent is sufficient for the condition to manifest. This condition results in red blood cells with a spherical shape, causing hemolysis (destruction of red blood cells).
Option C: Osteogenesis Imperfecta • Osteogenesis imperfecta is inherited in an autosomal dominant pattern. This genetic disorder affects the production of collagen, leading to brittle and easily fractured bones. It is caused by mutations in genes such as COLIA1 and COL1A2.
Option D: VWD (Von Willebrand disease) • Von Willebrand disease can be inherited in either an autosomal dominant or autosomal recessive manner, depending on the specific type of VWD. Some types are inherited dominantly, while others are recessively inherited. It's essential to determine the specific type to understand the mode of inheritance for an individual case.

stem: Patient presents with diarrhoea, abdominal pain and he develops these symptoms after consuming the foods such as wheat, rye, or barley. What is the pathology?
subject: Pathology, Medicine
explanation:
Solution
Cellac disease is an Immune-mediated disorder triggered by the ingestion of gluten-containing foods, such as wheat, rye, or barley, in genetically predisposed individuals. • When a person with Cellac disease consumes gluten, their immune system mounts an immune response against specific proteins found in gluten. One of the antibodies produced in response to gluten is antigliadin antibody, which is directed against gliadin, a component of gluten. This immune response can lead to damage in the small intestine, causing symptoms like diarrhea and abdominal pain,

Other Options:
Option B: Antimitochondrial antibody Antimitochondrial antibodies are associated with primary biliary cholangitis (PBC), a chronic autoimmune liver disease. They are not typically related to the symptoms of diarrhea and abdominal pain described in the scenario.
Option C: Anticentromere antibody • Anticentromere antibodies are more commonly associated with systemic autoimmune diseases like systemic sclerosis (scleroderma) and are not linked to the symptoms described.
Option D: Anti-topolsomerase antibody • Anti-topoisomerase antibodies are usually associated with systemic sclerosis (scleroderma), a connective tissue disease. They are not relevant to the presentation of diarrhea and abdominal pain.

stem: Patient with abdominal pain, IgA deposition, haematuria and purpura over leg as given in Image
subject: Pediatrics, Pathology
explanation:
Solution
Explanation: • Henoch-Schönlein Purpura This childhood syndrome consists of purpuric skin lesions, abdominal pain and intestinal. bleeding, and arthralgias along with rerial abnormalities. Skin lesions characteristically involve the extensor surfaces of arms and legs as well as buttocks; abdominal manifestations include pain, vomiting, and intestinal bleeding. A

Other Options:
Option A: IgA nephropathy IgA nephropathy is a kidney disorder characterized by the deposition of immunoglobulin A (IgA) antibodies in the glomerull of the kidneys. The glomerull are the tiny filtering units in the kidneys responsible for filtering waste and excess fluids from the blood to form urine. In IgA nephropathy, the deposited IgA complexes cause inflammation and damage to the glomerull, leading to blood and protein leakage into the urine, a condition known as hematuria and proteinuria, respectively. Over time, this condition can progress to chronic kidney disease (CKD).
Option C: Buerger's disease (Thromboangiitis Obliterans) Buerger's disease is a rare and non-atherosclerotic inflammatory condition that affects small and medium-sized blood vessels, particularly in the arms and legs. It is strongly associated with tobacco smoking. The inflammation leads to the formation of blood clots and subsequent blockage of the affected vessels, causing reduced blood flow to the extremities. This can result in pain, tissue damage, and in severe cases, gangrene. Buerger's disease does not typically involve the kidneys, so it is not associated with kidney-related symptoms like hematuria or proteinuria.
Option D: PSGN (Post-Streptococcal Glomerulonephritis) Post-streptococcal glomerulonephritis is a type of glomerulonephritis caused by an immune response triggered by a prior Infection with certain strains of streptococcal bacteria. The infection usually involves the throat (strep throat) or skin (impetigo). The immune system's response to the streptococcal infection leads to the deposition of immune complexes in the glomeruli, causing inflammation and impaired kidney function. PSGN is characterized by hematuria, proteinuria, high blood pressure, and sometimes swelling (edema). It most commonly affects children and young adults.

stem: MC cause of nephrotic syndrome in adults:
subject: Pathology, Medicine
explanation:
Solution
Explanation: Focal Segmental Glomerulosclerosis is the most common cause of nephrotic syndrome in adults. In FSGS, certain segments of the glomerull become scarred and damaged, leading to increased glomerular permeability and proteinuria. The term "focal" Indicates that only some of the glomeruli are affected, and "segmental" means that only a portion of each glomerulus is involved. FSGS can be primary (Idiopathic) or secondary to other conditions, and it is an important cause of chronic kidney disease.

Other Options:
Option A: Option A: Goodpasture Syndrome Goodpasture syndrome is a rare autoimmune disorder that primarily affects the lungs and kidneys. It is characterized by the presence of autoantibodies against the basement membrane of the glomeruli in the kidneys and alveoli (air sacs) in the lungs. The binding of these autoantibodies to the basement membrane triggers an inflammatory response, leading to damage In both organs. In the kidneys, it can cause glomerulonephritis, which may result in blood and protein leakage into the urine, leading to nephrotic syndrome. However, while Goodpasture syndrome can cause kidney-related problems, it is not the most common cause of nephrotic syndrome in adults.
Option B: Membranous GN (Glomerulonephritis) Membranous glomerulonephritis is a type of kidney disease that primarily affects the glomerull, the filtering units in the kidneys. In this condition, the glomerull become thickened and inflamed due to the deposition of immune complexes along the glomerular basement membrane. This immune response causes increased permeability of the glomerular filtration barrier, resulting in significant protein leakage into the urine, leading to nephrotic syndrome. • Membranous GN is indeed a common cause of nephrotic syndrome in adults.
Option C: Minimal Change Disease (MCD) Minimal Change Disease is a common cause of nephrotic syndrome in children. In MCD, there are subtle changes in the glomerull under the microscope, and the condition is often characterized by minimal or no visible abnormalities in kidney tissue on standard light microscopy. Despite this, it causes increased glomerular permeability and significant proteinuria, leading to nephrotic syndrome.

stem: Preferred biochemical markers in patients presenting with myocardial infarction:-
subject: Medicine, Pathology
explanation:
Solution
Explanation: The laboratory evaluation of Mi is based on measuring the blood levels of proteins that leak out of irreversibly damaged myocytes; previously, this involved measurement of the MB fraction of creatine kinase (CK-MB), Troponins are regulatory proteins found in cardiac muscle cells, and when there is damage to the heart muscle, such as during a heart attack, troponin is released into the bloodstream, Therefore, measuring the levels of cardiac troponins in the blood helps in diagnosing and confirming the occurrence of a heart attack.
• Currently, the most clinically useful biomarkers of myocardial damage are cardiac-specific troponins T and I (cTnT and cTnl), proteins that normally regulate calcium-mediated contraction of cardiac muscle.
• The diagnosis of myocardial injury is established when blood levels of troponin are elevated above threshold levels.

Other Options:
Option A: Option A: Myoglobin Myoglobin is a protein found in both skeletal and cardiac muscle cells. It is released into the bloodstream when there is muscle damage, including during a heart attack. Myoglobin levels may be elevated in the serum within one hour after myocardial cell death with peak levels reached within four to six hours. However, myoglobin is not as specific to the heart as troponins, as it can also be elevated in conditions that cause skeletal muscle Injury. Because of its lower specificity, myoglobin is not the preferred marker for diagnosing a heart attack, and It is usually used in conjunction with other markers, such as troponins, to provide a more comprehensive assessment.
Option C: CK-MB (Creatine Kinase-MB) CK-MB is an isoenzyme of creatine kinase found primarily in the heart muscle. Like troponins, its levels can rise in response to heart muscle damage, such as during a heart attack. In the past, CK-MB was widely used as a marker for myocardial infarction, but it has been largely replaced by troponins due to their higher sensitivity and specificity. CK-MB is no longer the preferred marker for diagnosing a heart attack, but it may still be used in certain situations.
Option D: All of the above • While all the options mentioned (troponins, myoglobin, and CK-MB) can be elevated in response to myocardial infarction. the preferred and most specific biochemical marker for diagnosing a heart attack is cardiac specific Troponins (Option B). Troponins have become the gold standard in clinical practice due to their high sensitivity and specificity for myocardial Injury. Therefore, Option D is not the correct answer.

stem: In beta thalassemia trait, electrophoresis shows:
subject: Pathology, Medicine
explanation:
Solution
Explanation: Beta thalassemia trait- Hemoglobin electrophoresis usually reveals an Increase in HbA2 (a262) to 4% to 8% of the total hemoglobin (normal, 2.5%=0.396) reflecting an elevated ratio of 6-chain to -chain synthesis. HbF levels are generally normal or occasionally slightly increased. A

Other Options:
Option A: Increased HbF & normal HbA2 This pattern is not characteristic of beta thalassemia trait. Increased levels of hemoglobin F (HbF), which is fetal hemoglobin containing gamma-globin chains, are more commonly seen in conditions like hereditary persistence of fetal hemoglobin (HPFH) or in certain genetic disorders affecting gamma- globin gene expression. Normal levels of hemoglobin A2 (HbA2) are not typical for beta thalassemia traits.
Option B: Normal HbF & normal HbA2 This pattern is not characteristic of beta thalassemia trait either. Normal levels of both hemoglobin F (HbF) and hemoglobin A2 (HbA2) would not be expected in beta thalassemia trait. Beta thalassemia trait is associated with a reduction in beta-globin chain production, leading to altered levels of HbF and HbA2 HbF generally reduces to a negligible amount by months of age.
Option D: Decreased HbF & normal HbA2 This pattern is not consistent with beta thalassemia traits. In beta thalassemia traits, HbF levels are usually not significantly decreased; instead, they can be normal or mildly Increased. As for HbA2, it is typically increased in beta thalassemia trait due to the excess alpha-globin chains forming HbA2 in the absence of sufficient beta-globin chains

stem: Which of the following is not a component of Jones criteria for diagnosing acute rheumatic fever?
subject: Medicine, Pediatrics
explanation:
Solution
Explanation:
RF is characterized by a constellation of major manifestations:
• Migratory polyarthritis of the large joints. Pancarditis (myocarditis, pericarditis, or endocarditis)
• Subcutaneous nodules (typically on extensor surfaces of extremities)
• Erythema marginatum, an irregular circinate skin rash
Sydenham chorea, a neurologic disorder with involuntary rapid movements.
Jones criteria: evidence of a preceding group A streptococcal infection, and the presence of two major manifestations, or one major and two minor manifestations (nonspecific signs and symptoms that include fever, arthralgia, or elevated blood levels of acute-phase reactants):

Other Options:
• Erythema nodosum is not a criteria for diagnosing acute rheumatic fever.

stem: HLA is located on:
subject: Microbiology, Pathology
explanation:
Solution
Explanation: HLA (Human Leukocyte Antigen) genes are located on the short arm of chromosome 6 (chr-6).
The HLA genes encode for the major histocompatibility complex (MHC) proteins, which are critical for the immune system's recognition of self and non-self antigens.
MHC proteins play a crucial role in presenting foreign antigens to immune cells, which is essential for immune responses and immune recognition of infected or abnormal cells.
These genes are highly polymorphic, meaning they have multiple alleles, contributing to the diversity of the immune response in different individuals.
• HLA typing is crucial for tissue and organ transplantation, as the compatibility of HLA types between donor and recipient is essential to reduce the risk of rejection.

Other Options:
Option B: Long arm of chr-6
This option is not correct.
HLA genes are located on the short arm, not the long arm, of chromosome 6.
Option C: Short arm of chr-3
• This option is not correct.
While chromosome 3 (chr-3) contains other important genes, the HLA genes responsible for encoding MHC proteins are located on the short arm of chromosome 6 (chr-6).
Chromosome 3 is one of the 23 pairs of chromosomes in humans.
People normally have two copies of this chromosome.
Chromosome 3 spans 201 million base pairs (the building material of DNA) and represents about 6.5 percent of the total DNA in cells.
Option D: Long arm of chr-3
This option is not correct.
As mentioned earlier, the HLA genes responsible for encoding MHC proteins are located on chromosome 6 (chr-6), not chromosome 3 (chr-3).

stem: A female with 20 weeks of pregnancy presents with fever and dysuria. A preliminary diagnosis of cystitis was made. Which of the following drugs will be safe to use for this patient?
subject: Pharmacology, Obstetrics and Gynaecology
explanation:
Solution
Explanation:
• Amoxicillin is safe to use in pregnancy and is a recommended antibiotic for treating cystitis during pregnancy.

Other Options:
Option A: Ciprofloxacin can cause joint problems in the fetus.
Option B: Gentamicin can cause hearing loss and kidney problems.
Option C: Cotrimoxazole can increase the risk of jaundice in newborns.

stem: The drug of choice for nasal carriers of MRSA is:
subject: Pharmacology, Microbiology
explanation:
Solution
• Mupirocin is a topical antibiotic used for eradication of nasal MRSA colonization.
It is applied as a nasal ointment (intranasal application).
• It works by inhibiting bacterial isoleucyl-tRNA synthetase, blocking protein synthesis.
• It is highly effective in decolonization protocols, especially in healthcare settings.
• Hence, it is the drug of choice for nasal MRSA carriers.

Other Options:
Option A: - Vancomycin
• Used for systemic MRSA infections.
Not used for nasal decolonization.
• Requires IV administration.
Option B: - Teicoplanin
Similar to vancomycin, used in systemic infections.
No role in nasal carriage eradication.
Option D: - Linezolid
• Used for serious MRSA infections.
• Oral/IV drug, not used for topical decolonization.

stem: What is the dose of mifepristone in Medical abortion?
subject: Pharmacology, Obstetrics and Gynaecology
explanation:
Solution
Mifepristone is an antiprogestin used in combination with misoprostol for medical abortion.
The recommended dose is 200 mg orally as a single dose.
It works by blocking progesterone receptors, leading to decidual breakdown and detachment of the gestational sac.
• This is followed by misoprostol to induce uterine contractions.
The 200 mg dose is as effective as higher doses and is the current standard protocol.

Other Options:
Option A: - 600 mg
• Previously used in older regimens.
• Found to be equally effective as 200 mg but with more cost and side effects.
• Hence, no longer standard.
Option C: - 400 mg
Not a recommended standard dose.
No added benefit over 200 mg.
Option D: - 100 mg
• Subtherapeutic dose.
• Not effective for medical abortion.

stem: Which of the following drugs causes postural hypotension commonly?
subject: Pharmacology, Medicine
explanation:
Solution
Explanation:
Postural hypotension is a condition where a person experiences a sudden drop in blood pressure when they stand up from a sitting or lying position. This can cause dizziness, lightheadedness, and even fainting.
Alpha-blockers are most commonly associated with causing postural hypotension.
Alpha-blockers are a class of drugs used to treat high blood pressure and certain other medical conditions, such as benign prostatic hyperplasia.
They work by relaxing the smooth muscles in blood vessel walls, which can cause blood vessels to dilate, resulting in a drop in blood pressure.

Other Options:
Option A:
ACE inhibitor - ACE inhibitors are a type of medication used to treat high blood pressure, heart failure, and other cardiovascular conditions.
While they can cause some side effects such as cough and dizziness, they are not typically associated with postural hypotension.
Option B: Beta blocker -
Beta blockers are another type of medication used to treat high blood pressure, heart failure, and other conditions.
• Like ACE inhibitors, they can cause side effects such as dizziness and fatigue, but they are not typically associated with postural hypotension.
Option D:
Angiotensin receptor blockers - Angiotensin receptor blockers (ARBs) are a type of medication used to treat high blood pressure and other cardiovascular conditions.
Like ACE inhibitors, they are not typically associated with postural hypotension.

stem: Mechanism of action of allopurinol is:
subject: Pharmacology, Biochemistry
explanation:
Solution
Explanation:
• Allopurinol is a medication commonly used to treat gout and hyperuricemia. It works by inhibiting the enzyme xanthine oxidase, which is involved in the breakdown of purines in the body.
Purines are a type of molecule found in many foods, and their breakdown leads to the formation of uric acid.
By inhibiting xanthine oxidase, allopurinol reduces the amount of uric acid produced by the body.
This can help to prevent the buildup of uric acid crystals in the joints, which can cause pain and inflammation characteristic of gout.

Other Options:
Option B: Recombinant uricase
Uricase is an enzyme that breaks down uric acid into allantoin, which is more soluble and easier to excrete.
However, humans do not produce uricase, so uric acid can accumulate in the body and lead to gout.
Recombinant uricase is a laboratory-produced version of the enzyme that can be used as a medication to break down uric acid in people with gout.
However, it is not as commonly used as allopurinol.
Option C: Increase uric acid excretion
Some medications, such as probenecid and lesinurad, work by increasing the excretion of uric acid in the urine, thus reducing the amount of uric acid in the blood and decreasing the risk of gout attacks.
• However, allopurinol works primarily by inhibiting xanthine oxidase, rather than increasing uric acid excretion.
Option D: Decrease chemotaxis
Chemotaxis refers to the movement of cells towards or away from a chemical signal.
While some medications may work by reducing the inflammatory response in gout, none of them work by specifically targeting chemotaxis.
Therefore, this option is not related to the mechanism of action of allopurinol.

stem: Which of the following drugs follow zero order kinetics?
subject: Pharmacology
explanation:
Solution
Explanation:
• Zero-order kinetics refers to a type of drug elimination where the rate of elimination is constant regardless of the concentration of the drug in the body.
In other words, the elimination of the drug is independent of its concentration.
• Phenytoin and aspirin are two drugs that follow zero-order kinetics. The elimination of phenytoin occurs through hepatic metabolism, and when the metabolic pathways are saturated, the drug is eliminated at a constant rate.
Similarly, aspirin is eliminated through a first-order process at low concentrations, but at higher concentrations, it follows zero-order kinetics.

Other Options:
Options C and D: On the other hand, morphine and propranolol follow first-order kinetics, where the rate of elimination is proportional to the concentration of the drug in the body.
Therefore, their elimination rate decreases as the concentration of the drug in the body

stem: Which of the following is an oral direct thrombin inhibitor?
subject: Pharmacology, Medicine
explanation:
Solution
Explanation:
Dabigatran is an oral direct thrombin inhibitor that works by inhibiting thrombin, an enzyme that plays a critical role in the blood clotting process.
It is used to reduce the risk of stroke and systemic embolism in patients with non-valvular atrial fibrillation, as well as to treat and prevent deep vein thrombosis (DVT) and pulmonary embolism (PE).

Other Options:
Option B:
Rivaroxaban, on the other hand, is an oral anticoagulant that belongs to the class of direct factor Xa inhibitors.
• It is also used to reduce the risk of stroke and systemic embolism in patients with non-valvular atrial fibrillation, as well as to treat and prevent DVT and PE.
Option C:
• Warfarin is an oral anticoagulant that works by inhibiting the synthesis of vitamin K-dependent clotting factors.
• It is used to prevent and treat blood clots in conditions such as atrial fibrillation, DVT, and PE.
Option D:
• Lepirudin is not an oral anticoagulant. It is a recombinant form of hirudin, a naturally occurring anticoagulant found in the saliva of medicinal leeches.
• It is used to treat patients with heparin-induced thrombocytopenia, a potentially life-threatening complication of heparin therapy.

stem: A lady has taken medication for amoebiasis infection. She drank alcohol at a party. She has nausea, vomiting and dizziness. Which anti-amoebic drug could have led to interaction with alcohol to produce these symptoms?
subject: Pharmacology, Medicine
explanation:
Solution
Explanation:
Metronidazole is an anti-amoebic drug commonly used to treat amoebiasis infection.
• One of the side effects of metronidazole is a disulfiram-like reaction when consumed with alcohol.
• Disulfiram is a medication used to treat alcohol dependence that produces unpleasant symptoms when alcohol is consumed.
The symptoms of a disulfiram-like reaction include nausea, vomiting, and dizziness, which are similar to what the lady is experiencing.

Other Options:
• Options B, C and D: Nitazoxanide, Paromomycin, and Diloxanide are also anti-amoebic drugs but do not have a known interaction with alcohol.

stem: Most potent opioid is:
subject: Pharmacology
explanation:
Solution
• Fentanyl is considered the most potent opioid out of the four options provided.
• Fentanyl is a synthetic opioid that is approximately 50-100 times more potent than morphine.
• It works by binding to the mu-opioid receptors in the brain and blocking pain signals, which produces a powerful analgesic effect.

Other Options:
Option B: Morphine is a naturally occurring opioid alkaloid that is commonly used for severe pain relief, but it is less potent than fentanyl.
Option C: Pentazocine is a synthetic opioid that is used for moderate to severe pain relief. However, it has a lower potency compared to fentanyl.
Option D: Pethidine, also known as meperidine, is a synthetic opioid that is used for moderate to severe pain relief. It has a potency similar to morphine but with a shorter duration of action.

stem: Most cardiotoxic anticancer drugs among the following:
subject: Pharmacology
explanation:
Solution
• Anthracyclines are the most cardiotoxic anticancer drugs. Anthracyclines are a class of chemotherapy drugs that are commonly used to treat a wide range of cancers, including breast cancer, lymphomas, and leukemias.
• Anthracyclines work by interfering with the DNA inside cancer cells, which prevents them from dividing and growing.
• However, they can also damage the DNA in healthy cells, including heart muscle cells, which can lead to cardiotoxicity.
• The exact mechanism of anthracycline-induced cardiotoxicity is not fully understood, but it is thought to involve the generation of free radicals and the inhibition of an enzyme called topoisomerase II, which is important for maintaining the structure and function of heart muscle cells.
• The cardiotoxic effects of anthracyclines can be acute, occurring during or shortly after treatment, or they can be delayed and appear months or even years later.
• Symptoms of cardiotoxicity can include shortness of breath, fatigue, chest pain, palpitations, and arrhythmias.

Other Options:
Option B: Cyclophosphamide - Cyclophosphamide is another chemotherapy drug used to treat various types of cancer, including lymphoma, leukemia, and breast cancer. Like anthracyclines, cyclophosphamide can also be cardiotoxic, particularly at high doses or with prolonged use. However, the incidence of cardiotoxicity with cyclophosphamide is generally lower than with anthracyclines.
Option C: Imatinib - Imatinib is a targeted therapy drug used to treat certain types of leukemia and other cancers. Unlike anthracyclines and cyclophosphamide, imatinib is not typically associated with cardiotoxicity.
Option D: Tamoxifen - Tamoxifen is a hormone therapy drug used to treat breast cancer. While tamoxifen is not typically considered cardiotoxic, it can increase the risk of blood clots and stroke, particularly in women with other risk factors such as smoking or high blood pressure.

stem: Which of the following is a carbonic anhydrase inhibitor?
subject: Pharmacology, Medicine
explanation:
Solution
Explanation:
• Acetazolamide is a carbonic anhydrase inhibitor. Carbonic anhydrase is an enzyme that catalyzes the conversion of carbon dioxide and water into bicarbonate and protons.
• Carbonic anhydrase is found in various tissues throughout the body, including the kidneys, lungs, and eyes.
• Acetazolamide works by inhibiting the action of carbonic anhydrase, which results in a decrease in the production of bicarbonate ions and an increase in the excretion of bicarbonate and sodium ions in the urine.
• This results in an alkaline diuresis, which can be useful in the treatment of conditions such as glaucoma, altitude sickness, and metabolic alkalosis.

Other Options:
Option B: Hydrochlorothiazide is a thiazide diuretic that works by inhibiting the reabsorption of sodium and chloride ions in the distal tubules of the kidneys.
Option C: Furosemide is a loop diuretic that works by inhibiting the reabsorption of sodium, chloride, and water in the ascending limb of the loop of Henle in the kidneys.
Option D: Mannitol is an osmotic diuretic that works by increasing the osmotic pressure of the blood, which results in an increase in urine production.

stem: Owl eye appearance of cells in urine examination of post kidney transplant patients is a feature of?
subject: Microbiology, Pathology
explanation:
Solution
• Owl's eye appearance is characteristic of CMV infection. Cytomegalovirus most commonly affects transplanted individuals.
• Intrauterine infection of CMV-fetal death or cytomegalic inclusion disease - leads to hepatosplenomegaly, microcephaly, jaundice, thrombocytopenic purpura, hemolytic anemia.
• Most important cause for microcephaly in children in CMV.
• Infection in children - mainly due to transfusion of CMV infected blood (Post transfusion mononucleosis).
• Heterophile, antibody negative, infectious mononucleosis - due to cytomegalovirus.

Other Options:
Option A: HSV - Herpes Simplex virus is a dsDNA virus that can cause herpes febrilis, Herpetic whitlow, Eczema herpeticum, Kaposi varicelliform, gingivostomatitis, HSV encephalitis. In Tzanck smear, it shows multinucleated giant cells.
Option B: HHV - Human Herpes virus has 8 types. HSV1, HSV2, Varicella zoster virus, Epstein Barr virus, Cytomegalovirus, Human B cell lymphotropic virus, RK virus and Rhadinovirus.
Option C: EBV - It is a type of Herpes virus that has B lymphocytes as the target cells. It causes infectious mononucleosis, oral hairy leukoplakia, Burkitt lymphoma, lymphoma in AIDS, Hodgkins lymphoma, Nasopharyngeal carcinoma, Anaplastic gastric carcinoma, T/NK cell lymphoma and Duncan's syndrome.

stem: Identify the organism stained by India ink dye and shown below?
subject: Microbiology, Pathology
explanation:
Solution
• Here the India ink stain in light microscopy shows capsulated yeast cells which are seen in Cryptococcus neoformans.
• Cryptococcus is a yeast fungus that has a capsule. The source of infection is mainly from pigeon feces and Eucalyptus tree which cause infection through inhalation.
• It presents as mild pneumonia as pulmonary cryptococcosis and can also cause meningitis in immunocompromised individuals like HIV infected patients, transplant patients.
• Most common cause of meningitis in AIDS patients with CD4 count <50 is Cryptococcus.
• The diagnosis is by doing a wet mount with India ink staining on CSF that shows the capsule. Serological cryptococcal antigen test by latex agglutination assay can be done. Culture is the gold standard.

Other Options:
Option B: Chlamydia is a obligate intracellular bacteria that can be seen in Giemsa stain.
Option C: Histoplasma is a dimorphic fungi.
Option D: Aspergillus is a fungi with septate hyphae.

stem: Identify the egg as shown in the image?
subject: Microbiology
explanation:
Solution
• The given image is diagnostic of pinworm. Enterobius vermicularis is also called threadworm or pinworm. Most commonly affects children.
• Infection is acquired by ingestion of embryonated eggs through hands, through inhalation of airborne eggs.
• Clinical features: Perianal pruritus (nocturnal itching) - because the worms come out, Abdominal pain and Excoriation.
• Laboratory diagnosis: Demonstration of eggs from anal swab/perianal scrapings which is done by scotch tape swab method using NIH swab.
