stem: A pregnant lady with a mood disorder was on lithium for her psychiatric issues. The baby born through her previous pregnancy had Down syndrome. This child can be born with which of the following abnormalities?
subject: Pharmacology, Obstetrics and Gynaecology, Pediatrics
explanation:
Solution
Lithium is a teratogenic drug.
Use of Lithium during pregnancy may cause Ebstein abnormality.
Ebstein abnormality -
It is a rare congenital heart disease.
In the Ebstein abnormality, the posterior and the septal leaflet of the tricuspid valve displaced downwards.
It is a cyanotic heart disease.
Clinical features -
1. History of cyanosis
2. Fatigue
3. Clubbing
4. Splitting of 1st sound
5. ECG shows prominent P waves
Treatment -
Surgical Treatment -
1. Obliterating the atrialised portion of the right ventricle.
2. Repairing of the tricuspid valve.

Other Options:
Option B: Neural tube defect:
Folic acid deficiency during pregnancy is the most common risk factor for neural tube defects.
Use of drugs like valproate and carbamazepine during pregnancy may increase the risk for neural tube defects.
Option C: Down Syndrome:
Risk factors -
1. Maternal age - Increase risk as mother's age increases.
2. Family history - Parents who have a child with Down syndrome have a slightly increased risk of having another child with the condition.
• The child can be born with down syndrome but use of lithium during pregnancy is more commonly associated with Ebstein abnormality.
Option D: Tetralogy of Fallot:
Risk factors -
1. Prenatal exposure of alcohol.
2. Maternal infections such as cytomegalovirus and rubella virus.
3. Drugs - thalidomide.

stem: A child with hypoglycemia was found to have burnt sugar urine odor. Which enzyme is deficient in this child?
options: Branched chain alpha keto acid dehydrogenase
subject: Biochemistry, Pediatrics
explanation:
Solution
The given clinical history is of maple syrup urine disease.
Burnt sugar urine odor is seen in Maple syrup urine disease.
It is an autosomal recessive disorder.
• Defect in branched chain alpha ketoacid dehydrogenase.
Elevated levels of plasma leucine, isoleucine, valine (VIL)
Clinical features:
Poor feeding
• Hypoglycemia
Drowsiness
Progressive encephalopathy
Hypertonia
Ketonuria
Irritability
Treatment:
Restriction of dietary substrate.
• Thiamine for milder form.
Orthotopic liver transplant.

Other Options:
Option B: Homogentisic acid oxidase-Enzyme deficient in alkaptonuria.
Urine turns black on standing (when come in contact with air).
Ear and nose cartilage (ochronosis) after 30 years.
Staining of diapers, gray discoloration of sclera.
Option C: Phenylalanine hydroxylase Enzyme deficiency in phenylketonuria.
It is an autosomal recessive disorder.
Musty body odor
• Reduced skin, hair and eye pigmentation.
Option D: Glucose 6 phosphatase
• It is a liver glycogen storage disease.
Manifest at 3-4 years of age with hypoglycemia, seizure, doll-like facies.
Hepatomegaly and growth retardation is seen.

stem: Which enzyme is deficient in Lesh Nyhan syndrome?
subject: Biochemistry, Pediatrics
explanation:
Solution
Lesh Nyhan syndrome
X-linked deficiency disorder
Clinical features:
Self mutilating behavior
• Developmental delay
Dystonia
Spasticity
Dysarthria
Elevated uric acid levels
Treatment:
• High fluid intake
Alkalinization
Allopurinol

Other Options:

Xanthine oxidase deficiency is seen in hereditary xanthinuria.
It is a rare autosomal recessive disorder.
Present with a large amount of xanthine in urine.
Treatment:
High fluid intake.
Low purine food
Alkalization

It is an inherited disorder in which the body is unable to recycle the vitamin biotin.

AST is the enzyme mostly found in the liver.

stem: A 2 year old girl presented with a history of recurrent episodes of early morning lethargy and seizures. On examination, the child had doll-like facies and hepatomegaly. Investigations revealed hypoglycemia, lactic acidosis and hyperlipidemia. Which enzyme is deficient?
subject: Biochemistry, Pediatrics
explanation:
Solution
The given clinical history is suggesting glucose 6 phosphatase deficiency.
It is a liver glycogen storage disease.
Manifest at 3-4 years of age with hypoglycemia.
Seizure,
Hepatomegaly and growth retardation is seen.
Kidneys may be enlarged.
Doll-like facies.
Liver histology shows: PAS positive, diastase sensitive glycogen and lipid laden vacuoles.
Diagnosis:
High uric acid (>5 mg/dl),
Elevated cholesterol and triglycerides.
Hypoglycemia (glucose <60 mg/dl),
Lactic acidosis.

Other Options:
Option B: Muscle phosphorylase
It is a type of glycogen storage disease.
Enzyme deficient in McArdle disease
Onset is in adolescence.
Clinical features:
• Exercise intolerance, Myoglobinuria, Muscle weakness, hypotonia, easy fatigability and cramps.
Option C: Glucose 6 phosphate dehydrogenase
X-linked recessive disorder
Characterized by hemolytic anemia on exposure to oxidative stress.
• Normally RBCs are protected from oxidant injury by reduced glutathione and G6PD is needed for production of reduced glutathione.
Both intravascular and extravascular hemolysis is seen.
Type 3 is the most severe form.
On peripheral smear- Heinz bodies, Bite cells, Spherocytes.
Option D: Hepatic Phosphorylase
Enzyme deficient in Hers disease.
Milder form of hypoglycemia and hepatomegaly.

stem: A 3 year old child presented with recurrent episodes of diarrhea and abdominal distension. On examination, he was found to have anemia, short stature and failure to thrive. An upper GI endoscopy along with duodenal biopsy revealed villous atrophy. What is the probable diagnosis?
subject: Pediatrics, Medicine, Pathology
explanation:
Solution
The clinical history suggests celiac disease (celiac sprue/gluten-sensitive enteropathy).
Celiac disease is a chronic malabsorption syndrome due to intestinal hypersensitivity to gliadin.
They are sensitive to barley, rye, oat and wheat (BROW).
Celiac disease may be associated with dermatitis herpetiformis.
Clinical features:
• Diarrhea
Anorexia
Weight loss
Failure to thrive
Deficiency of iron, folic acid and fat soluble vitamins (A,D,E,K)
Has a higher chance of enteropathy associated T-cell lymphoma
• Associated with type-1 diabetes mellitus
Diagnosis:
Based on histopathological findings:
1. Villous atrophy and crypt hyperplasia with decrease in villus crypt ratio.
2. Loss of brush border microvilli
3. Inflammatory cells present in lamina propria.
4. Overall mucosal thickness remains the same.
IgA antibodies to tissue transglutaminase
Anti endomysial antibodies.
Treatment: Gluten free diet.

Other Options:
Option A: It is characterized by ectasia of the bowel lymphatic system, which on rupture causes leakage of lymph in the bowel
Option C: Hirschsprung Disease aka Congenital aganglionic megacolon
It is caused by defective migration of neural crest cells into the mesodermal layer of the gut.
Results in intestinal dilatation proximal to the affected segment.
Rectum is always affected.
Present with delayed passage of meconium, bilious vomiting and abdominal distension.
On rectal biopsy:
Absence of ganglion cells and ganglia in affected segments.
Thickening and hypertrophy of nerve trunks
Option D: IBD is a chronic inflammatory disease of the GI tract and is of two types, Chron's and ulcerative colitis.

stem: Most common cause of inspiratory stridor in infant is:
subject: ENT, Pediatrics
explanation:
Solution
The most common cause of inspiratory stridor in infants is the laryngomalacia.
Laryngomalacia:
• Omega shaped epiglottis is seen on direct laryngoscopy.
Most common congenital abnormality of the larynx.
It happens because the supraglottic larynx, a floppy part of the larynx, has not yet developed the ability to maintain the airway rigidly.
These structures collapse into the airway when the larynx experiences negative pressure during inspiration.
Most children are symptom free by 1 to 2 years.
Treatment: reassurance, self limiting condition.

Other Options:
Option B: Parainfluenza
Laryngotracheobronchitis (croup)
Most common infectious cause of obstruction of upper respiratory tract in children.
cough/barking cough.
Inspiratory stridor.
Option C: Epiglottitis
H.influenzae is the most common causative organism.
It is the inflammation of epiglottis with inflammatory edema of hypopharynx
Muffled voice
Inspiratory retractions
• Soft stridor
Option D: Vocal cord Paralysis
It is the important cause of respiratory and feeding compromise in infants.
The causes of neonatal vocal cord paralysis are varied and include nervous system disorders, birth-related trauma, mediastinal masses, iatrogenic injuries and idiopathic causes.

stem: Vitamin K is given at birth to prevent the following:
subject: Pediatrics, PSM
explanation:
Solution
The condition of acute systemic bleeding and ecchymoses that emerge in the first week of life is known as hemorrhagic illness of the newborn, and it is most common in breastfed babies.
Bleeding from various sites including GIT, nasal, umbilical stumps or intracranial.
PT and aPTT are increased and normal platelet counts.
The main function of Vitamin K is to activate coagulation factor II, VII, IX and X.
Breast milk is deficient in vitamin K. Vitamin K biosynthesis occurs in the intestine and newborn do not have adequate intestinal bacterial flora to synthesize it so newborns are prone to vitamin K deficiency.
All healthy newborns should receive prophylactic vitamin K at birth in doses of 0.5-1.0 mg intramuscularly (IM) to prevent hemorrhagic illness.
Vitamin K deficiency can occur in:
Malabsorption syndrome.
GIT disease of newborn
Chronic antibiotics
Maternal use of drugs such primidone, warfarin and phenytoin.

Other Options:
Option B: Newborn diarrhea
Vitamin K administration at birth is not related to preventing newborn diarrhea. Newborn diarrhea can occur due to various factors, such as infections, formula intolerance, or feeding issues.
The treatment for newborn diarrhea involves addressing the underlying cause and ensuring adequate hydration.
Option C: Neonatal asphyxia
Vitamin K administration at birth is not related to preventing neonatal asphyxia. Neonatal asphyxia occurs when a baby does not receive enough oxygen before, during, or after birth.
It can have serious consequences and requires immediate medical attention and resuscitation.
Option D: Neonatal hypoglycemia
Vitamin K administration at birth is not related to preventing neonatal hypoglycemia.
Neonatal hypoglycemia is a condition where a newborn's blood sugar levels are abnormally low. It can occur in babies born to mothers with diabetes or other factors affecting glucose regulation.
Treatment involves closely monitoring blood sugar levels and providing appropriate interventions, such as feeding or glucose infusions.

stem: Given the dose-response curves for a drug as shown below, calculate the therapeutic index (TI) of the drug.
subject: Pharmacology
explanation:
Solution
• The therapeutic index is a measure of drug safety. It is the range of drug concentrations that can be administered safely and effectively.
• The therapeutic index can be calculated as the lethal dose of a drug for 50% of the population (LD50) divided by the minimum effective dose for 50% of the population (ED50),
i.e.
TI=LD50/ED50.
In the given problem,
median lethal dose = 400 mg
median effective dose = 100 mg
Hence, the therapeutic index =
400 mg / 100 mg = 4.

Other Options:
Option A: The option states that the therapeutic index is 1, which is wrong. The correct answer is 400 mg / 100 mg = 4.
Option B: The option states that the therapeutic index is 2, which is wrong. The correct answer is 400 mg / 100 mg = 4.
Option D: The option states that the therapeutic index is 8, which is wrong. The correct answer is 400 mg / 100 mg = 4.

stem: True regarding the dose-response curve shown in the image below?
options: C is more potent than A.
B is more potent than A.
subject: Pharmacology
explanation:
Solution
B is a competitive antagonist because it reduces the potency of drug A, but doesn't change the efficacy.
A competitive antagonist is a type of drug that binds to the same receptor site as an agonist but does not activate the receptor. Instead, the antagonist blocks the receptor site and prevents the agonist from binding, thereby reducing the effectiveness of the agonist.
• Potency is the amount of drug required for a given effect. It is represented by a 0.5 effect max value. The left shift in the curve indicates an increase in potency.
Potency: A=C>B

Other Options:
Option A: C is a non-competitive antagonist because it reduces the efficacy of drug A, but does not affect the potency.
Option B: C is equally potent as A. Potency is the amount of drug required for a given effect. It is represented by a 0.5 effect max value. The left shift in the curve indicates an increase in potency.
Option C: B is not more potent than A. Potency is the amount of drug required for a given effect. It is represented by a 0.5 effect max value. The right shift in the curve indicates a decrease in potency.

stem: The most effective antiemetic for chemotherapy-induced vomiting is?
subject: Pharmacology
explanation:
Solution
Granisetron is a serotonin 5-HT receptor antagonist developed to control cancer chemotherapy/radiotherapy induced vomiting. It is more potent than Ondansetron.

Other Options:
Option A: Doxylamine (along with pyridoxine) is used as first line treatment of morning sickness.
Option D: Domperidone is a D2 receptor antagonist used as an antiemetic for mild-to-moderate cases or postoperative, drug and disease-induced nausea and vomiting.
Option B: Tegaserod is one of the drugs prohibited for manufacture and sale by the Ministry of Health and Family Welfare, Govt. of India.

stem: What is the most common site of gastrinoma in MEN 1 syndrome?
subject: Surgery, Pathology
explanation:
Solution
• The first and second parts of the duodenum are the most typical sites for gastrinoma in MEN 1 disease.
• The parathyroid, pancreatic islet, and anterior pituitary tumours constitute the triad of tumours that make up the MEN 1 syndrome and are its defining features.
The most prevalent pancreatic islet tumour in MEN 1 syndrome is the gastrinoma.
It is known as Zollinger-Ellison syndrome when gastrinoma is accompanied by extensive stomach acid production and recurrent peptic ulcers.

Other Options:
Option A: Jejunum - The majority of small bowel cancer cases are adenocarcinomas, which often develop in the duodenum or jejunum. Adenocarcinoma starts in the small bowel gland cells.
Option B: Ileum - Small intestinal sarcoma is a leiomyosarcoma, a tumour that develops in the muscular tissue that makes up a portion of the gut. This form of tumour is most commonly found in the ileum.
Option D: Stomach - Adenocarcinoma of the stomach is the most common carcinoma at this site.

stem: A 50-year-old male who is a chronic smoker presented to the hospital with intermittent claudication pain in both thigh and buttock region on walking about 500 m. Which of the following is a likely diagnosis?
options: Arterial disease involving the superficial femoral artery.
Arterial disease involving the profunda femoris artery.
subject: Surgery, Medicine
explanation:
Solution
• Aorto-iliac involvement in arterial disease is suggested by the clinical scenario described.
• A characteristic of chronic arterial disease or artery occlusion is intermittent claudication. The muscle region that claudication typically affects is anatomically one level below the arterial disease.
• Thigh or buttock claudication may be brought on by aortoiliac disease.

Other Options:
Option A: Arterial disease involving the superficial femoral artery.
Option C:
• Femoral venous insufficiency - Femoral venous insufficiency is a syndrome caused by continuous chronic venous hypertension/ambulatory venous hypertension when standing or exercising (in normal people, venous pressure in the superficial system drops during calf contraction).
• Postural pain, varicose veins, oedema, pigmentation, induration, dermatitis, lipodermatosclerosis, and ulceration are symptoms of venous insufficiency.
• Venous insufficiency patients may have superficial vein incompetence with or without perforator incompetence, deep vein incompetence, or post-thrombotic syndrome.
Option D: Arterial disease involving the profunda femoris artery.

stem: A 42-year-old female presented with pain in the right upper quadrant on the fifth postoperative day following laparoscopic cholecystectomy. She is icteric. On ultrasound, there is fluid collection seen in Morrison's pouch. A biliary leak is suspected. Which is the most sensitive investigation to detect this pathology?
subject: Surgery, Radiology
explanation:
Solution
In this clinical scenario, the patient has developed symptoms and signs suggestive of a biliary leak after a laparoscopic cholecystectomy. Morrison's pouch fluid collection seen on ultrasound raises suspicion for this condition. To confirm the presence of a biliary leak and to determine its location, a HIDA scan is the most sensitive diagnostic test.
A HIDA scan involves injecting a radioactive tracer that is taken up by the liver and excreted into the biliary system. If there is a leak, the tracer will be seen outside the normal biliary system, indicating the site of leakage. This test is highly sensitive for detecting biliary leaks and is a valuable diagnostic tool in such cases.

Other Options:
Option A: ERCP (Endoscopic Retrograde Cholangiopancreatography) - While ERCP can be used to diagnose and treat biliary tract disorders, it is typically reserved for therapeutic interventions and may not be as sensitive as HIDA for detecting leaks.
Option B: MRCP (Magnetic Resonance Cholangiopancreatography) - MRCP is a non-invasive imaging technique that provides detailed images of the biliary system. While it can be helpful, it may not be as sensitive as HIDA for detecting small biliary leaks.
Option D: CECT (Contrast-enhanced Computed Tomography) - CECT can detect fluid collections and abnormalities in the abdomen, but it may not be as specific for diagnosing biliary leaks compared to HIDA.

stem: What is the diagnosis of IVP image shown in the figure?
subject: Surgery, Radiology
explanation:
Solution
The IVP image shows a classic appearance known as the "Cobra head" or "Adder head." This is indicative of a ureterocele.
• A ureterocele is a congenital abnormality found in the ureter. In this condition, the distal ureter balloons at its opening into the bladder, forming a sac-like pouch. It is most often associated with a duplicated collecting system, where two ureters drain their respective kidney instead of one. It's essential to recognize this condition in imaging studies as it can lead to various complications and may require surgical intervention.

Other Options:
Option A: Ureteric Calculus This is not the correct answer in this case. Ureteric calculi (stones) would appear differently on imaging, often as radiopaque or radiolucent shadows within the ureter.
Option C: Genitourinary TB This condition would have distinct radiological findings associated with it, such as calcifications and strictures in the urinary tract. The presented image doesn't match these characteristics.
Option D: Vesico Ureteric Reflux This condition is related to the backflow of urine from the bladder into the ureters. It typically doesn't present with the specific appearance seen in the image.

stem: A young male with head trauma is brought to the emergency in an unconscious state. He is intubated with a low Glasgow Coma scale, no verbal response and no movement on pressure and has features of increased ICT. NCCT Brain and spinal imaging are normal. What could be the likely diagnosis?
subject: Surgery, Radiology
explanation:
Solution
Diffuse Axonal Injury (DAI) is a type of traumatic brain injury that results from the acceleration-deceleration forces commonly seen in accidents like road traffic accidents.
DAI involves widespread damage to the axons in the brain, which disrupts neural connections.
• Importantly, DAI can occur even when there are no visible abnormalities on a CT scan.
• This injury can lead to various neurological impairments, including altered consciousness, cognitive deficits, and motor deficits.

Other Options:
Option A: Extradural hemorrhage, also known as epidural hematoma, typically occurs between the skull and the dura mater. It often presents with a lucid interval followed by rapid deterioration in consciousness. However, it's unlikely to have a normal CT scan in the presence of an epidural hematoma.
Option C: Post-concussion syndrome refers to a set of symptoms that can occur after a mild traumatic brain injury (concussion). In this scenario, the patient's low Glasgow Coma Scale score indicates a moderate level of impairment, which is not consistent with a mild concussion.
Option D: Brain herniation involves the displacement of brain tissue due to increased intracranial pressure. While traumatic brain injury can lead to brain herniation, the absence of raised intracranial pressure and normal CT scan findings make this diagnosis less likely in this case.

stem: A Child with bitemporal hemianopia. His IQ is normal. His visual acuity is diminished. The radiological image of this patient is shown below. What is the most likely diagnosis?
subject: Pediatrics, Radiology, Ophthalmology
explanation:
Solution
• The patient presented with bitemporal hemianopia, diminished visual acuity, and normal IQ. These clinical findings, along with the radiological image, are highly indicative of Craniopharyngioma.
Craniopharyngiomas are rare, usually suprasellar tumors. They are often partially calcified, solid, or mixed solid-cystic benign tumors that arise from remnants of Rathke's pouch. These tumors typically present in two age groups: children and adults between 55 and 65 years. The clinical symptoms include headaches, visual impairment, impaired growth in children, and hypopituitarism in adults.
• Radiologically, Craniopharyngiomas may show calcifications and are usually hypodense, which differentiates them from optic gliomas, which appear hyperdense on T2-weighted images.

Other Options:
Option A:
Pineal tumors are neoplasms that develop in or around the pineal gland, a small structure located deep within the brain.
• These tumors can be associated with a condition known as Parinaud syndrome, which includes symptoms like vertical gaze palsy, pupillary abnormalities, and nystagmus.
However, in the presented case, the patient's symptoms do not match the classic signs of Parinaud syndrome.
• Pineal tumors can also cause increased intracranial pressure, which may lead to symptoms such as headaches and nausea.
Radiologically, they are typically seen on imaging studies like MRI, showing their location in the pineal region.
Option B:
Torus tubarius is not a condition related to the central nervous system or visual abnormalities. Torus tubarius refers to a part of the auditory (Eustachian) tube found in the ear. It's not associated with the symptoms described in the case.
Option D:
Optic glioma is a type of tumor that arises from the glial cells of the optic nerve. These tumors can affect vision and may lead to optic atrophy, which can result in visual impairment.
However, the radiological features of optic gliomas typically show hyperdensity on T2-weighted images, which distinguishes them from Craniopharyngiomas. They can also be associated with neurofibromatosis type 1 (NF1).

stem: A 1-year-old child presented with anemia, hepatosplenomegaly, cervical lymph nodes and multiple scalp swellings with the given skull radiograph. He is diagnosed with multiple myeloma and has multiple lytic lesions on the skull X-ray. Which of the following drug is commonly used in this scenario?
subject: Pediatrics, Pharmacology
explanation:
Solution
• Vinblastine is commonly used in the treatment of certain types of leukemia (cancer of the white blood cells), including acute myeloid leukemia (AML, ANLL) and acute lymphoblastic leukemia (ALL), as well as Hodgkin's lymphoma (Hodgkin's disease) and non-Hodgkin's lymphoma.
In the context of pediatric multiple myeloma with lytic lesions on the skull X-ray, it can be part of the treatment regimen. Vinblastine is a chemotherapy drug that can help target and treat the cancerous cells in these conditions.

Other Options:
Option B: Vincristine is another chemotherapy drug used in the treatment of various childhood cancers, including certain types of leukemia and lymphoma. While it is an important chemotherapy agent, it is not typically the first-line treatment for multiple myeloma, especially in children.
Option C: Methotrexate is primarily used to treat inflammatory conditions such as arthritis and psoriasis. It is not typically the primary drug of choice for multiple myeloma, especially in pediatric cases.
Option D: Cyclosporine is an immunosuppressive agent used to treat organ rejection post-transplant. It is not a standard treatment for multiple myeloma, regardless of the patient's age.

stem: Which heart chamber compress the esophagus in patient with mitral stenosis?
subject: Anatomy, Medicine
explanation:
Solution
In patients with mitral stenosis, the left atrium is the heart chamber that compresses the esophagus. This occurs because mitral stenosis is characterized by the narrowing of the mitral valve orifice. Over time, the left atrium becomes enlarged as it works harder to push blood through the narrowed valve.
• This enlarged left atrium can exert pressure on adjacent structures, including the esophagus, leading to symptoms like dysphagia (difficulty swallowing), particularly with solid foods.

Other Options:
Option B: The right atrium is not directly involved in compressing the esophagus in mitral stenosis. The primary chamber affected is the left atrium.
Option C: The left ventricle is responsible for pumping oxygenated blood to the systemic circulation, and it is not directly associated with esophageal compression in mitral stenosis.
Option D: The right ventricle's main function is to pump blood to the pulmonary circulation, and it is not directly associated with esophageal compression in mitral stenosis.

stem: NCCT of paranasal sinus was shown in the image. What is the name of the sign shown?
subject: Radiology, ENT
explanation:
Solution
NCCT of the paranasal sinuses reveals a homogenous soft tissue opacity in the left maxillary antrum that extends into the nasal cavity. This is associated with the double density sign.
The double density sign appears as having a few hyperdense areas and thickened hypodense mucosa, which is suggestive of allergic fungal sinusitis.
On imaging, it typically presents as opacification and expansion of multiple paranasal sinuses, either unilaterally or bilaterally. The content within the sinus is centrally hyperdense on CT.
The majority of the sinuses appear nearly completely opacified, and unenhanced CT scans show this opacification with a centrally hyperdense material and a peripheral rim of hypodense mucosa.
In some cases, there may also be signs of expansion of the involved sinus, remodelling and thinning of the sinus wall, and erosion of the sinus wall.
This sign is considered "High Yield" for diagnostic purposes.

Other Options:
Option A: The "Onion peel appearance" is not associated with the given description. This term is commonly used to describe the appearance of smooth muscle tumors (leiomyomas) on ultrasound, where multiple concentric layers create a pattern resembling an onion.
Option B: The "Ground glass sign" is a term used in radiology but is more commonly associated with pulmonary imaging. It describes a hazy, ground glass-like appearance on CT scans of the lungs, often seen in conditions like interstitial lung disease.
Option C: "Honeycombing" is a radiological term typically used to describe the appearance of lung tissue in diseases such as idiopathic pulmonary fibrosis. It appears as a reticular pattern with small cystic air spaces, resembling a honeycomb.

stem: Identify the structure marked in the image?
subject: Anatomy
explanation:
Solution
The structure marked in the image is the "Cerebellum."
The cerebellum is the largest part of the hindbrain, located immediately inferior to the occipital and temporal lobes, within the posterior cranial fossa.
It plays a vital role in coordinating voluntary movements initiated by the cerebral cortex and is separated from the occipital and temporal lobes by the tentorium cerebelli.

Other Options:
Option B: The "Brainstem" is a separate region within the brain that includes the medulla oblongata, pons, and midbrain. It's responsible for various vital functions like breathing, heart rate, and consciousness.
Option C: The "Cerebrum" is the largest and most prominent part of the brain, responsible for higher cognitive functions like thinking, memory, and sensory processing. It's not the structure marked in the image.
Option D: The "Corpus callosum" is a bundle of nerve fibers connecting the two cerebral hemispheres. It's not the structure in the image; it's located deeper within the brain.
